The lock on the back door had been replaced three times in fifteen years. Not because it broke, but because Maren needed to know she was the only one with a key.

She turned it now, felt the mechanism click into place, then turned it again. Muscle memory. The kind that didn't require thought, only confirmation. Her fingers moved from the lock to the edge of the blinds, adjusted the angle by two degrees. The light that came through changed, became something she could tolerate.

The shop smelled of paper and dust and the particular mustiness of objects that had changed hands many times. She liked that smell. It meant things were where they should be. It meant the past was contained.

She ran her thumb along the edge of the counter. The wood there was worn smooth by years of her own touch. Her hand moved to the register, lifted it an inch, set it back down. Not checking. Just needing to know the weight of things.

Outside, the rain came down thin and grey, the kind that didn't announce itself. Maren watched it through the front window, the way it moved across the glass in patterns that seemed deliberate. The rain remembered what it touched on the way down. She'd learned that young, before she understood what remembering meant. Water carried the imprint of everything it passed through. That was why the Covenant conducted so much of its business in basements, in rooms without windows, in places where the water couldn't listen.

She pressed her palm flat against the glass. It was cold. Outside, a woman walked by with an umbrella that had a broken spoke, holding it at an angle that suggested she'd stopped caring about the rain a long time ago. Maren watched until the woman turned the corner and disappeared. Then she watched the corner for a while longer, in case she came back.

Her hands found the counter again. Tapped twice. Tapped once. The rhythm was unconscious, or it wasn't. Hard to tell anymore.

The bell above the door hadn't rung yet today. It was still early. The shop didn't open until nine, and it was not yet eight, and the rain was keeping people home, keeping them inside with their cups of tea and their small televisions, waiting for the sky to decide what it wanted to do. Maren had already had her tea. She'd had two cups, standing at the kitchen window of the apartment above the shop, watching the street below the way she did every morning. Counting the people she recognized. Noting the ones she didn't.

The new ones always made her pause. Just a moment. Just long enough to see where they were going, what they were looking at, whether their eyes moved the way eyes moved when they were looking for something specific. Most of them were nothing. People passing through. People who would never think about the shop again once they turned the next corner.

But some of them were something. And she had learned to treat all of them as possibly the latter.

She pulled the blinds down halfway. Not all the way, because that looked like she was hiding. Halfway looked like someone who preferred the dim light, someone who had a headache, someone ordinary.

The phone rang. She looked at it for three seconds before answering, which was two seconds longer than she usually waited. The number was local. Unfamiliar.

"Maren Cole."

"Mom."

The word sat in the air for a moment, taking up space. Maren closed her eyes.

"Elara."

"I wasn't sure you'd answer. I know it's early."

"I'm up." Maren moved the phone to her other ear. Her free hand found the counter, started its tapping again. "Everything all right?"

"Yeah. I just—" A pause. The kind that meant something was coming that Elara didn't know how to say. "I'm still at the hospice. Working the night shift this week."

"You didn't mention that."

"It was last minute. Dan needed someone."

Dan was the day supervisor, or had been. Maren hadn't spoken to him in years, but she remembered his voice, the way it went up at the end of every sentence like he was asking a question even when he wasn't. She remembered the way Elara had talked about him when she first started, the careful neutrality that meant she didn't want to say what she really thought.

"Night shift," Maren repeated.

"It's fine. It's quieter at night. Fewer people asking me questions about why I look like you."

The corner of Maren's mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. "What does that mean?"

"You know what it means. We have the same face, apparently. Mrs. Alderman told me I should smile more. That I'd be pretty if I smiled more. Like you."

"She said that to you?"

"Three times. She has dementia. I don't think she remembers saying it the first time."

Maren was quiet. Outside, the rain kept coming down, that patient persistence that defined the city for half the year. She thought about Mrs. Alderman, who was ninety-one if she was a day, who was dying by inches in a room that smelled of antiseptic and something older underneath, something that wasn't quite death but wasn't quite life either.

"You're good with them," Maren said. "The patients."

There was a silence on the other end. Not empty. Full of something Elara was deciding whether or not to put into words.

"They settle when I'm there." Elara's voice had changed. Softer. More careful. "I don't know how to explain it. It's like—they stop fighting. Whatever's keeping them awake at night, whatever's making them restless. They just... stop."

Maren's hand stopped tapping. She pressed her palm flat against the counter.

"That's a good skill to have."

"It's not a skill, Mom. It just happens."

The word *just* hung there. Maren heard what was underneath it—a question, maybe, or a fear, or something Elara didn't have language for yet. The mercury-thread. The thing that moved through certain bloodlines like a current through water, that allowed its carriers to reach into the threads of another person's body and smooth out the knots. The Covenant called it a gift. Maren had never been able to decide what she called it.

"Some people have a way with people," Maren said. "You're one of them."

"It's not—" Elara stopped. Started again. "Never mind. I just wanted to hear your voice. I don't know why."

"You working today?"

"Tonight. I'm off until five."

"Come by the shop. I'll make dinner."

A pause. Longer than the first one.

"I might. I don't know. Something came up."

Maren waited. The rain filled the silence, that soft percussion against the windows.

"It's nothing," Elara said. "I'll call you later."

The line went dead. Maren stood there with the phone still at her ear, listening to the absence of sound. Then she set it back in its cradle, slow and deliberate, the way she did everything.

Her hands found the counter again. Tapped twice. Tapped once.

She stood there for a while, in the dim light of the half-lowered blinds, listening to the rain and the silence and the space where her daughter's voice used to be.

*

The morning passed the way mornings did. She opened the shop at nine, stood behind the counter, watched people come and go. A man in a grey coat bought a lamp that had belonged to someone else's grandmother. A woman with a baby asked about antique mirrors, then changed her mind, then changed it back, then left with nothing. A boy who couldn't have been older than sixteen handled a pocket watch for ten minutes before putting it down and walking out without buying anything.

Maren watched them all. She watched the way they held things, the way they put them back, the way their eyes moved through the shop like they were looking for something specific or nothing at all. She watched the door when it opened and watched it again when it closed. She watched the street through the gaps in the blinds.

Her hands touched things constantly. The edge of the counter. The spine of a book. The brass handle of a drawer she never opened. The touching was unconscious, or it wasn't. She had stopped being able to tell the difference years ago.

At noon, she ate a sandwich she didn't taste, standing by the window. The rain had stopped at some point, but the sky was still the color of old concrete, still holding itself close. The street below was slick, reflecting the grey light in ways that made the cobblestones look like they were covered in something liquid and dark.

She thought about Elara. About the hospice. About the patients who settled when she was near, who stopped fighting, who found something like peace in the presence of a woman who didn't understand what she was.

The mercury-thread. It had been in her own family, generations back. Maren's grandmother had known how to find the knots, how to reach in and smooth them. Her mother had not inherited the gift—or had inherited something diluted, something that only worked on small things, minor aches, the everyday wounds that didn't require real power. Her father had been ordinary, as far as Maren knew. And Maren herself had never felt anything, never sensed the threads that everyone said were there, waiting to be touched.

But Elara was different. Elara had something, and it was getting stronger.

Maren pressed her forehead against the cold glass. She thought about calling someone, asking questions she didn't want to ask. She thought about Wen Trellyn, who would know more than she should, who would tell her things she didn't want to hear. She thought about Idris, who ran a tavern on the other side of the city and who had once been Void Clerk like her, who had walked away clean—or cleaner—and who might understand, or might not.

She thought about Dova Salter. The Arithmetician. The woman who held the ledgers, who knew the prices of everything, who had made a deal with Maren fifteen years ago and who was now making deals with Elara.

The phone rang.

Maren looked at it. The same local number. Elara's number, though Maren hadn't saved it, though she would have recognized it anyway.

She let it ring four times. Then five. Then she answered.

"Mom." Elara's voice was different now. Tight. The kind of tight that meant she was holding something back. "Something happened."

"Where are you?"

"At the hospice. I'm still at the hospice. It's not—I wasn't there when it happened. I came back and they told me. Mrs. Alderman. She died."

Maren closed her eyes.

"When?"

"This morning. Early. Before my shift ended. They said she just... stopped. In the middle of the night. They said she looked peaceful."

Peaceful. The word sat in the air, taking up space.

"That's how it happens sometimes," Maren said. "In places like that. People let go."

"I know. I know that. It's just—" Elara's voice broke. Not dramatically. Just a small fracture, a crack in the surface. "She kept asking for me. At the end. The night nurse said she kept saying my name. Like she was waiting."

Maren's hand found the counter. Tapped once. Tapped twice.

"She was ready," Maren said. "That's not a bad thing."

"I know."

"But you don't believe that yet."

A wet sound. Elara was crying, or trying not to. Maren could hear the effort it took, the way her daughter held herself together through force of will alone.

"No," Elara said. "I don't."

Maren stood there, phone pressed to her ear, listening to her daughter breathe. Outside, the sky was doing something strange—the light was changing, becoming thinner, more diffuse, as if the clouds were opening just a crack to let something through. The rain would come back. It always came back.

"Come to the shop," Maren said. "I'll make that dinner."

"I don't—"

"Elara."

A long silence. Then: "I'll be there in an hour."

The line went dead again. Maren set the phone down and stood very still.

In the window's reflection, she could see herself—the grey hair, the lines around her mouth, the eyes that had seen too much and learned not to show it. The scar along her left temple, white and precise, invisible unless you were looking for it.

She was looking for it now. She looked at it the way she looked at everything—assessing, cataloging, searching for what had changed.

Everything had changed. Everything was always changing. That was the only constant, in the end. The only thing you could count on.

Her hands found the counter. Tapped twice. Tapped once.

The rain came back, soft and patient, and Maren Cole stood at her window and watched it fall, the way it moved across the glass in patterns that seemed deliberate, the way it remembered everything it touched on the way down.
